explanation: The property specifies that whenever the system transitions from NOMINAL to TRANSITION state, this must occur only when the autopilot is not requesting support (i.e., when 'request' is false) at the moment of transition. This is modeled by detecting the precise moment when 'state_is_NOMINAL' is true and, in the next step, 'state_is_TRANSITION' becomes true, and ensuring that 'request' is false simultaneously.

The specification requires that if the autopilot is not requesting support (i.e., 'request' is False), then the sensor can transition from the NOMINAL state to the TRANSITION state. In LTL terms, this means that whenever 'state_is_NOMINAL' holds and on the next step 'state_is_TRANSITION' holds, it must be the case that 'request' was False in the original state. This is best modeled by: globally, if we see a transition from NOMINAL to TRANSITION, then 'request' must be False in the initial state.

The requirement states that the sensor transitions from NOMINAL to TRANSITION state when the autopilot is NOT requesting support (i.e., not request). We want to capture the moment where the state transitions from NOMINAL (true now, not true next step) and immediately in the next step TRANSITION is entered, and this only occurs when 'request' is false. Template 120 precisely encodes this: whenever state_is_NOMINAL becomes false (i.e., transition out of NOMINAL), then in the next step state_is_TRANSITION must hold, but this only applies when 'request' is not true. This fits the precise intent of the requirement.

This LTL property expresses that, whenever the 'request' signal is false (the autopilot is not requesting support), if the state is currently NOMINAL and will be TRANSITION in the next step, then this transition is permitted. In other words, the property detects rising edges on the transition from NOMINAL (now) to TRANSITION (next), given 'request' is false, and allows this behavior. This captures the requirement: the sensor may transition from NOMINAL to TRANSITION only when 'request' is not asserted (false).

This formula states that it is always the case that if the autopilot is not requesting support ('!request'), then if the system is currently in the NOMINAL state, it must be in the TRANSITION state in the next time step. This captures the requirement that a change of state from NOMINAL to TRANSITION is only possible when 'request' is not asserted, and it precisely defines the condition and the result over time.

The requirement is about transitions between states: specifically, when 'request' is false, the sensor can change from NOMINAL to TRANSITION. LTL does not directly support edge (state change) detection in boolean expressions, but we can encode a transition from NOMINAL to TRANSITION as: the system was in state_is_NOMINAL and in the next state it is in state_is_TRANSITION, i.e., (!state_is_TRANSITION & state_is_NOMINAL) at time t and (state_is_TRANSITION & !state_is_NOMINAL) at time t+1. However, due to template constraints, we focus on ensuring that whenever 'request' is false, the property 'if state_is_NOMINAL becomes false and state_is_TRANSITION becomes true in the next state' holds. This is most closely matched by the following monitor-style LTL, which checks for transitions: if (!request) and at the next step (state_is_TRANSITION & !state_is_NOMINAL), then at the current step you must not have already been in TRANSITION. The template 20 allows expressing that on the rising edge of '!request', at the next time step, (state_is_TRANSITION & !state_is_NOMINAL) holds. But since the boolean expressions must only use current time signals, we instead use another template to assert that whenever request is false, if in the current state we are not in TRANSITION yet, at the next state we will be in TRANSITION. Thus, the best fit is: G ((!request & state_is_NOMINAL & !state_is_TRANSITION) -> X state_is_TRANSITION)

The property specifies that whenever the system is not receiving a 'request' from the autopilot (i.e., !request), if a state change happens from NOMINAL to TRANSITION, this change occurs only under the condition of !request. The template captures the transition from 'state_is_NOMINAL' to 'state_is_TRANSITION' in the next time step, and it is guarded by the absence of the 'request'.

This LTL property expresses that globally, whenever the system is not being requested by the autopilot (i.e., 'request' is False), it is always the case that if the sensor state is currently NOMINAL, a state change to TRANSITION may only happen in the next step. The appropriate way to capture a state change from NOMINAL to TRANSITION is by observing that it is NOMINAL in the current step and TRANSITION in the next step. We encode this as: (!request) → (state_is_NOMINAL → X state_is_TRANSITION). This LTL formula directly captures the natural language requirement using the given atomic propositions.

The requirement specifies that the sensor shall change states from NOMINAL to TRANSITION when the autopilot is not requesting support (i.e., when 'request' is False). This is a property about the state transition happening from the NOMINAL state to the TRANSITION state at the next time step whenever the current state is NOMINAL and 'request' is False. Thus, we look for LTL expressing that whenever we move from 'state_is_NOMINAL' to 'state_is_TRANSITION', it must be the case that 'request' was False at the time of the transition. The template that best matches this is template 20: (G (((! bool_exp2) & (X bool_exp2)) -> (X bool_exp3))) & (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3), but flipping the logic to match the transition from NOMINAL to TRANSITION. So: when (!state_is_TRANSITION) & (X state_is_TRANSITION) (i.e., we are transitioning into TRANSITION), then in the previous state (now), 'state_is_NOMINAL' & '!request' must have been true. Thus, we set bool_exp2 = state_is_TRANSITION, and bool_exp3 = state_is_NOMINAL & (! request). Finally, the property ensures that such transitions only happen if the system was previously in NOMINAL and request was not asserted.

This property captures the requirement that a transition from the NOMINAL state to the TRANSITION state can only occur if the autopilot is not requesting support, i.e., 'request' is false. It does so by specifying that whenever the state changes from NOMINAL in the current step to TRANSITION in the next step, then 'request' must be false in the current step. The chosen template (120) enforces that for any such transition, the guard condition ('!request') holds at the point of transition.
